---
title: "Feature Engineering for Global ML Reconciliation"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Feature Engineering for Global ML Reconciliation}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

## Practitioners

The `level_id` flag in `terml_g()` and `ctrml_g()` appends a single integer column
to the stacked training matrix, encoding which temporal aggregation level each
training row belongs to. By default it is `FALSE` (off) to preserve backwards
compatibility.

**When to enable:** hierarchies with strong level-specific forecast error patterns.
Annual aggregates tend to have near-deterministic corrections; high-frequency leaves
often have large corrections. A global ML model without `level_id` cannot learn
these distinct regimes.

**Sparsity caveat:** On hierarchies with many structural zeros in the bottom layer,
enabling `level_id` does not change the NA handling behaviour. Missing observations
remain missing; `level_id` is a structural feature, not a data-imputation mechanism.

**Backwards compatibility:** fitting with `level_id = TRUE` produces an `rml_g_fit`
object with `ncol_hat` reflecting the wider feature matrix. Predictions on new base
data must supply the same number of feature columns. Attempting to predict with a
mismatched column count will raise a clear error.

### Quick start

```{r level_id_example, eval = FALSE}
library(FoRecoML)

# Construct a temporal hierarchy with agg_order = c(4, 2, 1)
# (annual, semi-annual, quarterly)
agg_order <- c(4L, 2L, 1L)
m  <- max(agg_order)       # 4 high-freq periods per annual cycle
kt <- sum(m / agg_order)   # 1 + 2 + 4 = 7 columns

T_obs <- 60L; h <- 2L

# Hat: T_obs rows × kt columns (one per temporal aggregation period)
hat <- matrix(rnorm(T_obs * kt), T_obs, kt,
              dimnames = list(NULL, paste0("L", seq_len(kt))))

# Obs: T_obs rows × 1 column (single bottom-level series)
obs <- matrix(rnorm(T_obs), T_obs, 1L,
              dimnames = list(NULL, "S1"))

# Base forecasts: h_hf rows × kt columns
base <- matrix(rnorm(h * m * kt), h * m, kt,
               dimnames = list(NULL, paste0("L", seq_len(kt))))

# Without level_id (default)
result_off <- terml_g(base = base, hat = hat, obs = obs,
                       agg_order = agg_order, seed = 1L)

# With level_id — model learns level-specific correction
result_on  <- terml_g(base = base, hat = hat, obs = obs,
                       agg_order = agg_order, level_id = TRUE, seed = 1L)
```

## Researchers

### Encoding rationale

`level_id` is encoded as an ordered integer, NOT as one-hot dummy variables.
Tree-based models (LightGBM, XGBoost, ranger) can recover threshold splits
from an ordered integer without the collinearity introduced by one-hot encoding.
A model using 6 one-hot columns for 6 temporal levels gains no additional
expressiveness over the single ordered integer, but wastes 5 feature columns.

### Reference

The encoding is motivated by empirical findings in Yingjie & Abolghasemi (2024,
arXiv:2411.06394), who show that cross-hierarchy features including level identity
are critical for global ML models to differentiate level-specific correction magnitude.

### Ordering convention

`level_id = 1` corresponds to the finest temporal granularity (smallest `k` in
`agg_order`, e.g. monthly in a monthly/quarterly/annual hierarchy). This is the
**reverse** of `FoReco::FoReco2matrix`'s list index ordering, where `parts[[1]]`
corresponds to the coarsest aggregation.

The derivation is `rank(match(kset, sort(kset)))`, which assigns rank 1 to the
smallest `k` value (finest granularity).
